reasoning: The passage of comprehensive tech legislation in the US Congress typically takes many years of debate, consensus-building, and negotiation, as seen with federal data privacy and antitrust efforts which have failed to pass for over a half-decade. While there is bipartisan interest, sweeping regulation of AI is opposed by a strong industry lobbying effort aiming to preserve American innovation, and current congressional leadership frameworks focus more on research and funding than binding rules. Furthermore, the legislative calendar (including the 2024 elections and the transition to a new Congress in 2025) severely restricts the window for passing highly complex, binding regulatory mechanisms on a novel technology by the end of 2025. Given the strict resolution criteria requiring substantive mandatory standards or prohibitions, the probability of enactment before Jan 1, 2026 is very low.
